probate-attorney-upper-kirby-houston-tx.heroIntro: The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has helped Upper Kirby families navigate Harris County probate and estate planning for over 25 years. Whether you're administering an estate, contesting a will, or putting a plan in place to protect your home and assets, we work with you directly — attorney to client.
probate-attorney-upper-kirby-houston-tx.localContent:
Upper Kirby sits in the heart of Houston's Inner Loop, just south of River Oaks and west of Greenway Plaza, anchored by the Kirby Drive corridor and the dining and shopping along West Alabama, Westheimer, and Richmond. It's a neighborhood of high-rise condos, mid-rise townhomes, and pocket single-family streets — and that mix shapes the kind of probate and estate planning issues we see for clients here. Many Upper Kirby residents own their primary home through an HOA or condominium association, hold investment property elsewhere in Texas, and have a mix of brokerage and retirement accounts. Each of those assets transfers differently at death, and getting them aligned in advance is one of the most useful things an estate plan can do.

Because Upper Kirby is in the City of Houston and Harris County, probate matters for residents are heard by one of the four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — at 201 Caroline Street downtown. Cases are randomly assigned among the four courts, and each judge runs a slightly different docket. Knowing the local procedures of each court is part of how we keep uncontested probate moving efficiently for our Upper Kirby clients.

If you need to probate a will, open an heirship proceeding when there is no will, contest a will you believe was signed under undue influence, or put a plan in place that keeps your home and accounts out of court entirely, we'd like to help. Call us at (281) 609-0303 or request a free case review — we'll tell you honestly whether you need a lawyer at all.

Probate Services for Upper Kirby Residents

Probate is the court-supervised process of settling a deceased person's estate. We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Inner Loop families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal court administration with or without an independent executor
  • Muniment of Title — a faster, lower-cost alternative when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage and only needs title transferred
  • Determination of Heirship — used when there is no valid will, to establish legal heirs under Texas intestate succession
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors and administrators on their legal duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold ($75,000 in non-real-property assets)

Estate Planning for Upper Kirby Families

A well-built estate plan keeps your family out of probate court whenever possible, minimizes delays, and ensures your wishes are honored if you can't speak for yourself. We help Upper Kirby residents put together plans that include:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful when you own property outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — transfer your home outside of probate, which is especially common among Inner Loop condo owners

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Harris County

If you believe a will was signed under undue influence, lack of testamentary capacity, or fraud, we represent contestants and proponents in will contests at the Harris County Probate Courts. We also represent beneficiaries who suspect a trustee or executor has breached fiduciary duty — failing to account, self-dealing, or misappropriating estate assets.

Life Insurance Disputes

Life insurance carriers sometimes deny or delay legitimate beneficiary claims, particularly when contestability period issues, beneficiary designation disputes, or interpleader actions arise. We pursue contested life insurance claims for Upper Kirby families and others throughout Southeast Texas — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Upper Kirby and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout the greater Houston area, including Upper Kirby, River Oaks, Greenway Plaza, Montrose, the Museum District, and all surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. When you work with us, you work directly with an attorney — not a paralegal or case manager — from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which Harris County Probate Court will hear my case if I live in Upper Kirby?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County has four statutory probate courts — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 — and your case is randomly assigned at the time of filing. All four sit at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, about a ten-minute drive from Upper Kirby. The case is heard by whichever court draws the assignment; you don't choose. We handle filings in all four courts regularly.

localFaqs.question: I own a condo in Upper Kirby — does it have to go through probate when I die?
localFaqs.answer: Not necessarily. If you hold the condo jointly with right of survivorship, or if you've recorded a Texas Transfer on Death Deed naming a beneficiary, title can pass outside of probate. If you hold it solely in your name with no TOD deed and no living trust, your beneficiaries will need probate (or a Muniment of Title proceeding if the estate has no unpaid debts other than the mortgage) to transfer title. We can review your deed and tell you exactly which path applies.

localFaqs.question: How long does an uncontested probate take in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: For an uncontested independent administration in Harris County, expect roughly 4 to 8 months from filing to closing — depending on the docket of the assigned probate court, complexity of the estate, and whether all heirs cooperate. A Muniment of Title proceeding is often faster, sometimes 2 to 4 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: I'm an out-of-state executor and the deceased lived in Upper Kirby. Do I need to come to Houston?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes for the initial probate hearing, but Harris County probate courts now allow many subsequent appearances by Zoom. We routinely represent out-of-state executors and can serve as the registered agent in Texas, handle filings, attend hearings, and coordinate with you remotely so your travel is limited to what's strictly necessary.

probate-attorney-downtown-houston-tx.heroIntro: Downtown Houston is the heart of Harris County — and home to the four probate courts where every probate matter for residents of Houston's central core is heard. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has filed and resolved probate cases at 201 Caroline Street for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-downtown-houston-tx.localContent:
Downtown Houston has changed dramatically over the last two decades. Where there were once mostly office towers, residential life has returned in force — Skyhouse, Market Square Tower, One Park Place, the Houston House, the Star, and the lofts in the Theater and Warehouse Districts now house thousands of residents. With that residential growth has come a recurring set of probate questions: how does a high-rise condo transfer? What about a parking deed? What if I work in oil and gas and have stock options that vested but didn't pay out? We see these issues regularly for downtown clients.

The good news for Downtown residents is that the courthouse is right there. The four Harris County Statutory Probate Courts sit at 201 Caroline Street, in the Civil Courthouse just north of Discovery Green. Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 each have their own judge and docket; cases are randomly assigned at filing. Whether you live in a Theater District loft, a Market Square high-rise, or a Midtown townhome, your case will land in one of those four courts.

If you've recently lost a family member, are serving as executor of a Downtown resident's estate, or want to put a plan in place that keeps your assets out of probate, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Downtown Houston Residents

Probate is the court-supervised process of settling a deceased person's estate. We handle the full range of Texas probate work for Downtown clients:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration in any of the four Harris County probate courts
  • Muniment of Title — for estates with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage; common for downtown condo owners
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will, to establish legal heirs under Texas law
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Downtown Houston Residents

Downtown residents often have a particular asset profile — a condo, brokerage and retirement accounts, sometimes employer stock or RSUs, and frequently family or property in another state. A solid estate plan ties all of those together. We help Downtown clients with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — especially useful for residents who own real estate outside Texas
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your condo or townhome out of probate entirely

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in the Harris County Probate Courts. If you believe a will was signed under undue influence, lack of capacity, or fraud — or if a trustee or executor is mishandling estate assets — there's a two-year statutory window to contest, and earlier action almost always strengthens the case.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims on behalf of beneficiaries — denied claims, contestability disputes, interpleader actions — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Downtown and Inner Loop Houston

Our office serves clients throughout Downtown Houston, Midtown, EaDo, the Museum District, and all surrounding Inner Loop neighborhoods. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Downtown Houston residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 are all located at 201 Caroline Street in downtown Houston, in the Harris County Civil Courthouse just north of Discovery Green. Cases are randomly assigned to one of the four courts at filing.

localFaqs.question: I live in a downtown high-rise condo. Does it have to go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on how the title is held. A condo titled solely in the deceased's name with no Transfer on Death Deed will need probate or a Muniment of Title proceeding to transfer. A condo held jointly with right of survivorship, or covered by a recorded Texas Transfer on Death Deed, can transfer outside of probate. Reviewing your deed and beneficiary designations now is one of the simplest steps in estate planning.

localFaqs.question: How quickly can I get a probate matter heard in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Initial probate hearings in Harris County are typically scheduled 2 to 4 weeks after the application is filed, depending on which of the four probate courts the case is assigned to. After the hearing, an uncontested independent administration generally closes in 4 to 8 months.

localFaqs.question: Are downtown condo HOAs ever a complication in probate?
localFaqs.answer: They can be. HOA fees and assessments continue to accrue during probate, and most condo associations require notice of the owner's death and proof of executor authority before they'll communicate with the family. We help executors navigate that notice process and protect against late fees or liens during administration.

probate-attorney-sugar-land-tx.heroIntro: Sugar Land sits at the heart of Fort Bend County — one of the most diverse and high-income counties in Texas. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has guided Sugar Land families through Fort Bend probate and estate planning for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-sugar-land-tx.localContent:
Sugar Land has grown from a sugar refinery town into one of the most prosperous and diverse cities in Texas. Today the city is built around master-planned communities — First Colony, Sweetwater, Greatwood, Riverstone, Telfair, Avalon, Sienna (just south, in Missouri City) — many of them home to professionals, business owners, and families with significant assets to plan around. Fort Bend County has consistently ranked among the highest-income counties in the state, and that shapes the kind of estate planning we do for Sugar Land residents: more frequently we're dealing with multi-state assets, business interests, charitable giving, and blended families.

Probate matters for Fort Bend County residents are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. Filings are made through the Fort Bend County Clerk. Fort Bend's docket tends to move faster than Harris County's, and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months for our Sugar Land clients.

If you need to probate a will in Fort Bend County, open an heirship proceeding for a relative who died without a will, contest a will or trust, or build out an estate plan that protects your home, business, and family, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Sugar Land Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters for Fort Bend County families:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2
  • Muniment of Title — a streamlined option for Sugar Land homeowners when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will, to establish legal heirs under Texas intestate succession
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Sugar Land Families

Sugar Land's professional and business-owner population often needs a more layered plan than a simple will. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — especially useful for clients with property outside Texas, business interests, or blended families
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps Sugar Land homes out of probate
  • Special Needs Trusts and charitable giving structures, where appropriate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Fort Bend County

We litigate will contests, trust contests, and fiduciary breach claims in Fort Bend County. If you believe a parent's will was signed under undue influence, or a co-trustee is mishandling assets, the two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate — earlier action protects more options.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims on behalf of Sugar Land beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Sugar Land and Fort Bend County

Our office serves clients throughout Sugar Land, Missouri City, Stafford, Richmond, Rosenberg, Pecan Grove, Greatwood, Riverstone, Sienna, First Colony, Telfair, Aliana, Cinco Ranch, and the surrounding Fort Bend communities. When you work with us, you work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which court hears probate matters for Sugar Land residents?
localFaqs.answer: Probate cases for Fort Bend County residents — including Sugar Land — are heard by Fort Bend County Court at Law No. 2 at the Justice Center, 301 Jackson Street in Richmond. The probate division phone is (281) 341-4443. Filings are made through the Fort Bend County Clerk.

localFaqs.question: Is probate faster in Fort Bend County than in Harris County?
localFaqs.answer: Generally yes. Fort Bend's docket carries a smaller volume than Harris County's four probate courts combined, and uncontested independent administrations often close in 3 to 6 months in Fort Bend, compared with 4 to 8 months in Harris. Complex or contested cases take longer in either county.

localFaqs.question: Sugar Land has master-planned communities with HOAs — does that affect probate?
localFaqs.answer: HOAs themselves don't change probate procedure, but assessments continue to accrue during administration, and most associations require notice of the homeowner's death and proof of executor authority before they'll communicate with heirs. We help Sugar Land executors handle that notice and protect against late fees or liens.

localFaqs.question: I'm a Sugar Land resident with property in another state. Do I need a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Real property in another state typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" in that state if it's titled in the deceased's individual name — meaning your family runs two probates instead of one. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that. We can review your situation and tell you whether a trust makes sense.

probate-attorney-conroe-tx.heroIntro: Conroe is the county seat of Montgomery County — and home to Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1, where every probate matter for the county is heard. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Conroe families and executors at the Lee G. Alworth Building for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-conroe-tx.localContent:
Conroe has been one of the fastest-growing cities in Texas for the better part of a decade. The city anchors the north end of the Houston metro, just east of Lake Conroe and north of The Woodlands, and its growth has brought a wide range of residents — retirees who chose the area for the lake and the lower cost of living, younger families in master-planned communities like Grand Central Park, and longtime Montgomery County families with land that has been in the family for generations. Each of those groups brings a different set of probate and estate planning needs to our office.

The advantage of practicing probate in Conroe is that the courthouse is right downtown. Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1 sits at 210 West Davis Street in the Lee G. Alworth Building — one of only a handful of dedicated statutory probate courts outside of Texas's largest urban counties. That means probate matters here are heard by a full-time probate judge with a focused docket, and uncontested cases generally close in 4 to 6 months.

Whether you're administering an estate, dealing with a will contest, or putting a plan in place for the next generation, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Conroe Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — independent or dependent administration
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — for estates with no will, common for longtime Montgomery County families with inherited land
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Conroe Families

A clear estate plan can keep your home and assets out of court entirely. We help Conroe residents with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for retirees with property in multiple states
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — common for Conroe homeowners and lakefront property
  • Special Needs Trusts, where appropriate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation in Montgomery County

If you believe a will was signed under undue influence — a recurring issue with elderly relatives in caregiving situations — or that a co-trustee or co-executor is breaching duty, we represent contestants and proponents in Montgomery County Probate Court. The two-year statutory contest window matters; earlier action almost always strengthens the case.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Conroe families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Conroe and Montgomery County

Our office serves clients throughout Conroe, Willis, Magnolia, Porter, New Caney, Oak Ridge North, Shenandoah, Montgomery, and the surrounding Montgomery County communities. You work directly with an attorney from your first consultation through the resolution of your matter.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where do I file probate for a Conroe resident?
localFaqs.answer: Probate cases for Montgomery County residents are filed with the Montgomery County Clerk and heard by Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1, located at 210 West Davis Street in the Lee G. Alworth Building in downtown Conroe. The court phone is (936) 539-7832.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Montgomery County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Montgomery County Probate Court No. 1 generally close in 4 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months. Contested cases take significantly longer.

localFaqs.question: We have land in the family that's been passed down for generations — is probate different?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Long-held Montgomery County family land where successive owners died without probating their wills sometimes requires multiple Determinations of Heirship to clear title — one for each generation that didn't go through probate. It's solvable, but it takes more work than a routine administration. We handle these cases regularly and can map out exactly what's required to clear title to inherited land.

localFaqs.question: I retired to Lake Conroe from another state. Do I need a new will?
localFaqs.answer: Your existing will is likely valid in Texas if it was validly executed under your prior state's law, but Texas has unique rules — the option of independent administration, community property treatment of marital assets, the Transfer on Death Deed — that an older out-of-state will rarely takes advantage of. We typically recommend a Texas-specific will or trust update once you've established residency here.

probate-attorney-pearland-tx.heroIntro: Pearland sits primarily in Brazoria County, with smaller portions in Harris and Fort Bend. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Pearland probate matters in all three jurisdictions and has represented Brazoria County families for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-pearland-tx.localContent:
Pearland has grown into one of the largest suburbs in the Houston metro, anchored by master-planned communities like Shadow Creek Ranch and Silverlake along with established neighborhoods on the Brazoria County side. Most of Pearland sits in Brazoria County, but a smaller portion of north Pearland is in Harris County, and a sliver to the west is in Fort Bend. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's actual physical address at the time of death — not the ZIP code or mailing address. We'll confirm jurisdiction at your first consultation.

For the Brazoria County majority of Pearland residents, probate matters are heard by Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court at the Brazoria County Courthouse, 111 East Locust Street in Angleton — about a 25-minute drive south of central Pearland. Filings are made through the Brazoria County Clerk. Brazoria's docket tends to move efficiently, and uncontested probate often closes in 3 to 6 months.

If you're administering an estate, contesting a will, or putting an estate plan in place, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Pearland Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Brazoria, Harris, and Fort Bend County probate courts:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in the appropriate county court
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Pearland homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Pearland Families

A clear plan keeps your family out of court whenever possible. We help Pearland residents with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for clients with property outside Texas or blended families
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Pearland home out of probate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Brazoria, Harris, and Fort Bend County probate courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Pearland beneficiaries — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Pearland and the South Houston Communities

Our office serves clients throughout Pearland, Silverlake, Shadow Creek Ranch, Manvel, Iowa Colony, Friendswood, Alvin, Meridiana, Angleton, and surrounding South Houston communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Pearland is in three counties — which one handles probate for my family member?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the deceased's legal residence at the time of death. Most of Pearland is in Brazoria County, with smaller portions in Harris and Fort Bend. We confirm the correct county at your first consultation by checking the property records and the deceased's address. Filing in the wrong county wastes time and money — getting it right at the start matters.

localFaqs.question: Where is the Brazoria County probate court?
localFaqs.answer: Brazoria County Court at Law No. 4 and Probate Court is located at the Brazoria County Courthouse, 111 East Locust Street, Room 201, Angleton, TX 77515 — about a 25-minute drive south of central Pearland. Filings are made through the Brazoria County Clerk.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Brazoria County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Brazoria County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 3 months. Contested cases take longer.

localFaqs.question: We bought our Shadow Creek Ranch home with my parents on the deed — does it still need probate when they die?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the form of co-ownership. If the deed includes "with right of survivorship" language, title passes to the surviving owners without probate. If the deed is just joint tenancy or tenancy in common with no survivorship language, the deceased parent's interest must pass through probate or by Muniment of Title. We can review the deed for you in a free consultation.

probate-attorney-galveston-tx.heroIntro: Galveston is an island city with its own jurisdictional realities — historic homes, beachfront condos, hurricane-affected properties, and a probate court right at the county seat. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented island families and out-of-state property owners for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-galveston-tx.localContent:
Galveston is unlike any other community in the Houston metro. The island has historic homes in the East End Historic District, beachfront condominiums along Seawall Boulevard, working-class neighborhoods, vacation rentals owned by people who live somewhere else entirely, and a tourism economy that brings the population up dramatically on summer weekends. Each of those housing patterns shapes the kind of probate work we do for Galveston clients. We see beneficiaries inheriting beach condos they don't want to keep, families clearing title on hurricane-damaged property that's been in the family for generations, and out-of-state heirs trying to administer an estate they've never set foot in.

The advantage for Galveston residents is that the courthouse is right on the island. Galveston County Probate Court sits at 600 59th Street, Suite 2305 in Galveston, in the Galveston County Courthouse. The court hears probate, guardianship, and mental health matters for the entire county — covering Galveston, League City, Friendswood, Texas City, Dickinson, Kemah, La Marque, and the surrounding Bay Area.

If you've inherited property on the island, are administering an estate, or want to put a plan in place that protects your home from lengthy court delays, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Galveston Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Galveston County Probate Court:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Galveston County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Galveston homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — common for older Galveston families with property whose chain of title was never cleared after a death
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Ancillary Probate — for out-of-state owners of Galveston beach property whose primary probate is happening elsewhere

Estate Planning for Galveston Property Owners

Many Galveston homeowners — especially beachfront condo owners — live somewhere else most of the year. A solid plan ties Texas property to your overall estate plan. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — especially useful for non-resident owners of Galveston property, to avoid an ancillary Texas probate
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Galveston home or condo out of probate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and fiduciary breach claims in Galveston County Probate Court. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Galveston families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Galveston and the Bay Area

Our office serves clients throughout Galveston, League City, Friendswood, Texas City, Dickinson, Kemah, Seabrook, Nassau Bay, Webster, La Marque, and the surrounding Bay Area communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the Galveston County Probate Court?
localFaqs.answer: Galveston County Probate Court is located at 600 59th Street, Suite 2305, Galveston, TX 77551, on the second-floor of the Galveston County Courthouse. The court phone is (409) 766-2251.

localFaqs.question: I live out of state but inherited a beach condo on the island — what do I do?
localFaqs.answer: If your relative's primary probate is happening in another state, the Texas property typically requires a separate "ancillary probate" in Galveston County to transfer title. We routinely represent out-of-state heirs and can serve as Texas registered agent, file the ancillary proceeding, and coordinate with your home-state attorney. If the relative had a revocable living trust holding the condo, that ancillary probate is often avoidable.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster — sometimes 2 to 3 months — if the estate qualifies.

localFaqs.question: Hurricane Ike damaged our family home decades ago and we never cleared title after my grandfather died. Can we still fix it?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship and, if needed, a series of heirship proceedings going back through the chain of title. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable. We handle these cases for Galveston families regularly.

probate-attorney-league-city-tx.heroIntro: League City is the largest city in Galveston County and a hub of the Bay Area. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented League City families and Bay Area aerospace, maritime, and energy professionals through Galveston County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-league-city-tx.localContent:
League City has grown into the largest city in Galveston County and one of the most active suburbs in the Houston metro. The community sits between Houston and Galveston Island, anchored by the Clear Lake area, with strong ties to the aerospace industry through NASA and the Johnson Space Center, the maritime and energy industries through the Bay Area refineries, and increasingly to remote workers who chose League City for the schools and quality of life. Each of those professional groups brings a different mix of compensation — pensions, RSUs, deferred comp, retirement accounts — that an estate plan needs to account for.

League City sits in Galveston County, so probate matters for League City residents are heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston — about a 25-minute drive south of League City. The court phone is (409) 766-2251 and filings are made through the Galveston County Clerk.

If you're administering an estate, dealing with a contested will, or building out an estate plan that ties together complex compensation, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for League City Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Galveston County Probate Court:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Galveston County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for League City Families

League City professionals often have layered compensation that needs careful coordination. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families and clients with property in multiple states
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your League City home out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for retirement accounts, pensions, and life insurance — areas where we frequently see avoidable mistakes

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Galveston County Probate Court. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Serving League City and the Clear Lake Bay Area

Our office serves clients throughout League City, Friendswood, Clear Lake, Webster, Nassau Bay, Seabrook, Kemah, Dickinson, Galveston, Texas City, La Marque, and surrounding Bay Area communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Which court hears probate matters for League City residents?
localFaqs.answer: League City sits in Galveston County, so probate cases are filed with the Galveston County Clerk and heard by Galveston County Probate Court at 600 59th Street in Galveston. The court phone is (409) 766-2251.

localFaqs.question: My spouse worked at NASA and had a federal pension. Does it go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: Federal pensions and most other retirement accounts pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Problems arise when the named beneficiary is an ex-spouse, has predeceased, or was never updated. Reviewing your beneficiary designations is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for Bay Area professionals.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Galveston County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Galveston County typically close in 3 to 6 months from filing. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster, sometimes 2 to 3 months.

localFaqs.question: We have a vacation home in Galveston and primary home in League City. Two probates?
localFaqs.answer: If both properties are in Galveston County and you die a Texas resident, one probate handles both. If your vacation property is in another state — say, a beach house in Florida — that property typically triggers a separate "ancillary probate" there, on top of the Texas probate. A revocable living trust holding the out-of-state property usually avoids that.

probate-attorney-katy-tx.heroIntro: Katy sits at the intersection of three counties — Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's actual address, not the ZIP code. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC handles Katy probate matters in all three jurisdictions with over 25 years of experience.
probate-attorney-katy-tx.localContent:
Katy is one of the more legally complicated communities in the Houston metro because it sits at the intersection of three counties. Most of Katy and the master-planned communities around it — Cinco Ranch, Cross Creek Ranch, Firethorne, Cane Island, Falcon Landing — sit in either Harris County or Fort Bend County, with smaller pockets in Waller County to the northwest. A Katy ZIP code (77449, 77450, 77493, 77494) tells you nothing definitive about which county a particular home sits in. Where probate is filed depends on the deceased's legal residence at the time of death, confirmed by checking the actual property records.

That makes the first step in any Katy probate matter the same: confirm the correct county. We file in all three:

  • Harris County — Probate Courts No. 1, 2, 3, and 4 at 201 Caroline Street, Houston
  • Fort Bend County — Court at Law No. 2 at 301 Jackson Street, Richmond
  • Waller County — Constitutional county court at 836 Austin Street, Hempstead

Filing in the wrong county wastes time and filing fees, and can complicate notice to creditors. Getting the jurisdiction right at the start matters. If you're administering a Katy resident's estate, contesting a will, or planning ahead, give us a call at (281) 609-0303 for a free case review and we'll confirm the correct county before any filing.

Probate Services for Katy Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters across Katy's three counties:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in the appropriate county court
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option for Katy homeowners with no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — when there is no valid will
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Katy Families

A clear plan keeps your home and assets out of court whenever possible. We help Katy residents with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — particularly useful for energy-sector professionals with multi-state assets
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Katy home out of probate regardless of which county it's in

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in all three Katy-area county courts. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Katy families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Katy and the West Houston Suburbs

Our office serves clients throughout Katy, Cinco Ranch, Cross Creek Ranch, Firethorne, Fulshear, the Energy Corridor, and surrounding West Houston communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: I live in Katy — which county handles my probate?
localFaqs.answer: Katy spans three counties: Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller. Your ZIP code doesn't determine the county; the property records do. We confirm jurisdiction at the first consultation by checking the deceased's address against county property data. Most of Cinco Ranch is in Fort Bend; older Katy north of I-10 is largely in Harris; communities in northwest Katy can be in Waller.

localFaqs.question: What if probate was filed in the wrong county?
localFaqs.answer: It can be transferred, but the time and filing fees are wasted, creditor notices have to be re-issued, and the heirs lose weeks or months on the timeline. That's why confirming the correct county before filing matters. If you've already filed in the wrong county, we can help with the transfer and minimize the disruption.

localFaqs.question: How does the timeline differ between Harris, Fort Bend, and Waller for Katy residents?
localFaqs.answer: Harris County typically takes 4 to 8 months for an uncontested independent administration. Fort Bend tends to run 3 to 6 months. Waller, being a smaller rural county, often moves faster — 3 to 5 months. The county where the deceased lived determines which timeline applies.

localFaqs.question: I work in oil and gas and have RSUs and stock options. Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: It depends on the equity plan. Vested stock that's been transferred to a brokerage account passes by beneficiary designation if one is named, otherwise through probate. Unvested RSUs and unexercised options are usually governed by the plan document — many plans accelerate vesting at death and pay to the named beneficiary, but plans differ. We review equity grant agreements as part of estate planning for Katy energy-sector clients.

probate-attorney-mont-belvieu-tx.heroIntro: Mont Belvieu is the heart of Chambers County's energy economy. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Mont Belvieu families and petrochemical industry professionals through Chambers County probate for over 25 years.
probate-attorney-mont-belvieu-tx.localContent:
Mont Belvieu has grown rapidly along with the Gulf Coast petrochemical industry. The city sits along I-10 east of Baytown and is anchored by the underground salt-dome storage facilities and processing plants of Enterprise, Targa, and other major operators — making it one of the most economically significant small cities in Texas. Many Mont Belvieu families work directly in the petrochemical sector, often with substantial 401(k) balances, employer stock, deferred compensation, and pension benefits that need careful coordination in any estate plan. The newer master-planned communities — Eagle Creek, Magnolia Creek, Champions Estates — bring younger families with growing assets and a different planning profile than longtime Chambers County landowners.

Mont Belvieu sits in Chambers County, so probate matters are heard by the Chambers County Court at the courthouse in Anahuac, 404 Washington Avenue — about a 15-minute drive east of Mont Belvieu. The county also operates a branch annex in Mont Belvieu at 10616 Eagle Drive for many County Clerk services, which can be convenient for filing certain documents. Chambers is a smaller rural county and uncontested probate matters typically close in 3 to 5 months — often faster than Harris or Fort Bend.

Whether you're administering an estate, dealing with a contested will, or building a plan that protects your home, retirement, and family, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Mont Belvieu Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Chambers County Court:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Chambers County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — common for longtime Chambers County families with land that has passed informally between generations
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Mont Belvieu Families

Petrochemical industry professionals often have layered compensation that needs careful coordination with the rest of the estate plan. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for blended families, multi-state property, and complex compensation
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your Mont Belvieu home out of probate
  • Beneficiary designation review for 401(k)s, pensions, and life insurance — areas where we frequently see avoidable mistakes

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Chambers County. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Mont Belvieu families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Mont Belvieu and Chambers County

Our office serves clients throughout Mont Belvieu, Anahuac, Old River-Winfree, Cove, Beach City, Baytown, and surrounding East Houston communities. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the probate court for Mont Belvieu residents?
localFaqs.answer: Mont Belvieu is in Chambers County, so probate cases are heard by the Chambers County Court at the courthouse in Anahuac, 404 Washington Avenue. The county phone is (409) 267-2400. Chambers County also operates a branch annex at 10616 Eagle Drive in Mont Belvieu for many County Clerk services, including some filings.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Chambers County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested independent administrations in Chambers County typically close in 3 to 5 months — often faster than the larger metropolitan counties because dockets are smaller. A Muniment of Title proceeding can be faster still, sometimes 2 to 3 months.

localFaqs.question: I work for a petrochemical company and have employer stock plus a 401(k). Do those go through probate?
localFaqs.answer: 401(k)s and most retirement accounts pass by beneficiary designation, not by will — meaning they bypass probate entirely if a valid beneficiary is named. Employer stock held in a brokerage account follows the same rule if there's a beneficiary designation; if it's held without one, it goes through probate. Reviewing your beneficiary designations is one of the simplest and highest-value pieces of estate planning we do for Chambers County industry professionals.

localFaqs.question: We have land that's been in our family for generations — is probate different?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes. Long-held Chambers County family land where successive owners died without probating their wills sometimes requires multiple Determinations of Heirship to clear title — one for each generation that didn't go through probate. It's solvable, but it takes more work than a routine administration. We handle these heirship cases for Chambers County families regularly.

probate-attorney-hempstead-tx.heroIntro: Hempstead is the county seat of Waller County — and where every Waller County probate matter is heard. The Law Office of Troy M. Moore, PLLC has represented Waller County families with over 25 years of Texas probate experience.
probate-attorney-hempstead-tx.localContent:
Hempstead is the county seat of Waller County, sitting along U.S. 290 about an hour northwest of Houston. The community has long been an agricultural hub — Hempstead is sometimes called the "Watermelon Capital of Texas" — and Waller County remains one of the more rural counties in the Houston metro, with cattle ranches, timber land, and family farms still defining much of the landscape. That shapes the kind of probate work we do for Waller County clients. We see more inherited land, more multi-generational title issues, and more cases where probate has been informally skipped for decades and now needs to be cleaned up before the next sale or transfer.

Probate in Waller County is heard by the Waller County Court — the constitutional county court — at the courthouse, 836 Austin Street in Hempstead. Filings are made through the Waller County Clerk in the same building. Waller's docket is smaller than the metropolitan counties, and uncontested probate matters typically close in 3 to 5 months.

Whether you're administering an estate, clearing title on inherited land, or putting a plan in place for the family farm, we'd like to help. Call (281) 609-0303 for a free case review.

Probate Services for Hempstead and Waller County Residents

We handle the full range of Texas probate matters in Waller County Court:

  • Probate of a Last Will and Testament — formal administration in Waller County
  • Muniment of Title — efficient option when the estate has no unpaid debts other than a mortgage
  • Determination of Heirship — frequently used for Waller County family land where probate was never opened
  • Independent and Dependent Administration — guidance for executors on duties to creditors, heirs, and the court
  • Small Estate Affidavits — for qualifying estates under the statutory threshold

Estate Planning for Waller County Families

For families with land, livestock, and farm equipment, planning is about more than just a will. We help with:

  • Last Will and Testament
  • Revocable Living Trusts — useful for keeping family land out of probate and structuring how it passes to the next generation
  • Durable Power of Attorney
  • Medical Power of Attorney and Advance Directives (Living Will)
  • Texas Transfer on Death Deeds — keeps your home or family land out of probate
  • Special Needs Trusts and family land arrangements, where appropriate

Will Contests and Fiduciary Litigation

We litigate contested wills and breaches of fiduciary duty in Waller County. The two-year statutory contest window starts at the opening of probate.

Life Insurance Disputes

We pursue contested life insurance claims for Waller County families — often on a contingency basis.

Serving Hempstead and Waller County

Our office serves clients throughout Hempstead, Prairie View, Waller, Brookshire, Pattison, and surrounding rural communities in Waller and west Harris County. You work directly with an attorney from start to finish.

Probate and estate matters are often emotionally difficult. Our goal is to handle the legal complexities so you can focus on what matters most to your family.

localFaqs.question: Where is the Waller County probate court?
localFaqs.answer: Waller County probate matters are heard by the constitutional county court at the Waller County Courthouse, 836 Austin Street, Hempstead, TX 77445. Filings are made through the Waller County Clerk at the same address. The clerk's office phone is (979) 826-7711.

localFaqs.question: Our family has land that's been passed down without probate — can we still clear title?
localFaqs.answer: Yes, almost always. Title issues caused by deaths that were never probated can be resolved through a Determination of Heirship — and where multiple generations have died without probate, sometimes a series of heirship proceedings is needed. It takes more work than a routine probate, but it's solvable, and we handle these cases for Waller County families regularly.

localFaqs.question: How long does probate take in Waller County?
localFaqs.answer: Uncontested probate in Waller County typically closes in 3 to 5 months. Smaller rural counties often move faster than metropolitan Harris because the docket carries less volume.

localFaqs.question: We own a working farm. Should we put it in a trust?
localFaqs.answer: Often yes — particularly when more than one heir will inherit, or when the operation needs to continue uninterrupted after a death. A revocable living trust holding the land, equipment, and livestock can avoid probate entirely and keep the operation running through the transition. We can sit down with you and walk through whether a trust makes sense for your specific land and family circumstances.
